title: Handling Errors: 2> and &>
description: Separate error messages from normal output. Combine or split streams like a pro.

slides.title: Redirecting stderr: `2>` 🚫
slides.content:
# Silence Those Annoying Errors

`>` only captures stdout. If your command produces errors, they will still clutter your terminal or get lost.

To redirect **only** stderr, use `2>`.

```bash
find / -name "*.conf" 2> errors.log
```

- **stdout** (found files) prints to the terminal.
- **stderr** (permission denied) is saved to `errors.log`.

### Discarding Errors Entirely:
Send them to the black hole `/dev/null`.
```bash
find / -name "*.conf" 2> /dev/null
```
This is the cleanest way to run a command when you don't care about the error noise.

slides.title: Capture Only Errors
slides.content: List a non-existent file and a real file, capturing only the error.

quizData.question: To redirect error messages to a file called `err.log`, you use `command _____ err.log`.
quizData.explanation: stderr is file descriptor 2, so the operator is `2>`.

slides.title: Sending stderr to the Same Place as stdout 🔀
slides.content:
# The Classic `2>&1`

Often you want to capture **both** normal output and errors into the **same** file for a complete log.

You need to redirect stderr (2) to wherever stdout (1) is currently going.

**Correct Syntax:**
```bash
command > output.log 2>&1
```

**Order Matters!** This does NOT work:
```bash
command 2>&1 > output.log   # WRONG!
```

### Why Order Matters:
- `2>&1` means "Make stderr point to **whatever stdout is pointing to RIGHT NOW**."
- If you put `2>&1` first, stderr points to the **terminal** (original stdout). Then you move stdout to the file. stderr is left behind, still pointing to the terminal.

slides.title: Capture All Output
slides.content: Redirect both stdout and stderr to a single file.

slides.title: The Shorthand: `&>` (Bash Only) 💨
slides.content:
# Quick and Dirty Combination

Bash provides a convenient shortcut for `> file 2>&1`.

```bash
command &> combined.log
```

This redirects **both** stdout and stderr to `combined.log`.

### Append Both:
```bash
command &>> combined.log
```

**Note:** `&>` is a **Bash extension**. It works in Bash and Zsh but not in plain `/bin/sh`. For maximum portability in scripts, stick to `> file 2>&1`.

quizData.question: Which command correctly sends both stdout and stderr to `output.txt`?
quizData.explanation: The order is critical: redirect stdout first, then redirect stderr to the new destination of stdout.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Separate Logging for Debugging

Advanced scripters often log stdout to one file and stderr to another, then use `tail -f` on the error log to watch for problems while the script runs.
